one_line_takeaway: A unified multi-agent framework and 3B-token benchmark showing that across 49 LLMs no jailbreak defense wins on all of safety, cost, and utility simultaneously, and that even sophisticated safety judges disagree enough (Cohen's Kappa ~0.4 among LLM judges) to shift the conclusions.
key_finding: No single defense is optimal across all dimensions, and there is a fundamental three-way trade-off among safety, compute cost, and utility. Empirically: (1) safety does not reliably improve with recency or scale, variance across newer models actually grows, and newer families (Qwen3) can be less safe than older ones (Qwen2.5); proprietary models (Claude, GPT-4o) show much lower ASR (<3% without defense) than some open models (DeepSeek-R1, Qwen3-1.7B >20%). (2) Among attacks, RandomSearch is strongest (avg ASR 24%), followed by AIM (15%) and PastTense (13%); template attacks remain surprisingly potent. (3) Defenses cut ASR by roughly one-third to one-half but at real cost: SmoothLLM incurs up to 5x baseline token usage and strong defenses (e.g., SelfDefense) degrade utility by up to 25% AlpacaEval winrate; Paraphrase is a balanced middle ground. (4) Judges disagree substantially: rule-based vs LLM judges have Cohen's Kappa of only 0.071-0.126, and even LLM judges agree only moderately (Kappa ~0.405-0.453 between GPT-4o/Qwen2.5/Llama3.3), so judge choice materially changes safety conclusions.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PANDAGUARD, a modular, plugin-based framework that conceptualizes LLM jailbreak safety as a four-component multi-agent system (attackers A, target models M, defenders D, safety judges J) with the formal objective min over M,D of E_P[J(D(M,A(P)))]. The framework implements 19 attack methods, 12 defense mechanisms, and multiple judge strategies behind consistent abstraction interfaces (attack(), defense(), judge(), generate()/batch_generate()) driven by YAML configuration, and supports 7 LLM backends (HF Transformers, vLLM, SGLang, Ollama, OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini APIs) plus four CLI use-cases (chat, serve, inference, attack). On top of it the authors build PANDABENCH, described as the largest-scale reproducible jailbreak evaluation to date: 49 open- and closed-source LLMs evaluated across the attack/defense/judge combinations, consuming ~3 billion tokens. The paper's own contribution is the unified framework + benchmark + empirical cross-component analysis, not the individual attacks/defenses (GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, RandomSearch, SmoothLLM, SelfReminder, Jailbreak Antidote, etc.), which are re-implementations of prior cited work integrated under one standardized protocol.
threat_model: Adversary submits carefully engineered prompts (jailbreaks) to a deployed, safety-aligned LLM to bypass alignment guardrails and elicit harmful, biased, or unethical outputs (e.g., malware/hacking instructions, fraud, disinformation, physical-harm guidance). The paper spans both black-box attackers (no parameter access, e.g., PAIR, AutoDAN, template attacks like AIM/BETTER_DAN) and white-box/optimization attackers with gradient or query access (e.g., GCG, RandomSearch). Defenders sit on model input or output (input filtering, prompt manipulation/paraphrasing, in-context reminders, representation-level adjustment, post-generation evaluation) and aim to reduce Attack Success Rate while preserving utility and controlling compute cost. Safety judges (rule-based refusal detection and LLM-based scoring) are the measurement layer, and the paper explicitly treats judge unreliability as a first-class concern rather than a fixed oracle.
limitations: The authors state the framework is text-only and does not cover multimodal (image/audio/combined) jailbreaks, which they flag as the next frontier. They acknowledge judge unreliability introduces inherent variance in their own outcomes and that harm definitions are subjective across contexts/cultures; they plan human-evaluation studies to ground what a 'true' jailbreak is. Additional reviewer-noted constraints: the benchmark uses only 100 prompts (JBB-Behaviors, 10 per category), so per-category ASRs rest on small samples; adaptive attacks all share one proxy (Llama-3.1-8B), which aids fairness but may not reflect a stronger real adversary; and the headline counts are inconsistent between framework and benchmark (19 attacks/12 defenses implemented, but PANDABENCH section reports evaluating 19 attacks and 9 defenses).

datasets_used: JBB-Behaviors from JailbreakBench (100 harmful prompts, 10 categories x 10 prompts each) - evaluation prompt set
AlpacaEval winrate - utility/quality measurement of defended models
PANDABENCH (authors' own released benchmark: 49 LLMs, 19 attacks, ~3 billion tokens)
quantitative_results: Scale: 49 LLMs, 19 attack methods, 12 defense mechanisms (PANDABENCH section states 9 defenses evaluated), 4 judges, 7 LLM interfaces, ~3 billion tokens total
Attack effectiveness (avg ASR): RandomSearch 24%, AIM 15%, PastTense 13%
Model vulnerability: Claude-3.5 and GPT-4o <3% ASR without defense; DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3-1.7B >20% ASR without defense
Defense impact: defenses reduce ASR by ~one-third to one-half
Defense cost: SmoothLLM up to 5x baseline token usage; strong defenses (e.g., SelfDefense) degrade utility by up to ~25% AlpacaEval winrate
Judge agreement (Cohen's Kappa): rule-based (GCG) vs LLM judges 0.071-0.126; among LLM judges (GPT-4o / Qwen2.5-72B / Llama3.3-70B) ~0.405-0.453 (moderate)
Per-category ASR (with defense) examples: malware/hacking 11.5%, fraud/deception 17.9%, harassment/discrimination 17.9%, disinformation 4.5%
ASR success threshold: PAIR criterion, judge score = 10 required
baselines_compared: Framework comparison (Table 1): JAILJUDGE (2 attacks/3 defenses/18 judges/4 models), EasyJailbreak (12/0/7/10), AISafetyLab (13/16/7/1), HarmBench (18/0/3/33) vs PANDAGUARD (19/12/4/49)

Attack methods benchmarked against each other: RandomSearch, AIM, PastTense, AutoDAN, ReNeLLM, BETTER_DAN, GCG, PAIR, TAP, DeepInception, GPTFuzz, ArtPrompt, SCAV, ICA, COLD, GPT4Cipher, FutureTense, ANTI_GPT_V2, DEV_MODE variants, Baseline
Defense methods benchmarked: Paraphrase, Semantic SmoothLLM, SmoothLLM, SelfDefense, GoalPriority, SelfReminder, ICL, RPO, PerplexityFilter vs Baseline (no defense)
Judges compared: rule-based (GCG refusal detection) vs LLM-based GPT-4o-11-20, Qwen2.5-72B-it, Llama3.3-70B-it (PAIR-style scoring)
